name: Port of Palombera and Alto de San Cipriano from Ruente – circular through the Saja-Besaya Natural Park
name_original: Puerto de Palombera y Alto de San Cipriano desde Ruente – circular por el Parque Natural Saja-Besaya
region_intro: Road cycling around Ruente, nestled in Cantabria's Saja Valley, offers diverse terrain characterized by green mountains, lush forests, and open meadows. The region is part of the Saja-Besaya Natural Park, featuring significant elevation changes and winding roads through protected natural areas. Cyclists can navigate routes along the Saja River and explore plateaus, providing a varied landscape for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Ruente The most popular road cycling route is Port of Palombera and Alto de San Cipriano from Ruente – circular through the Saja-Besaya Natural Park, a 69.2 miles (111.3 km) trail that takes 5 hours 38 minutes to complete. This challenging circular route features significant climbs through the Saja-Besaya Natural Park. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Los 10,000 del Soplao Road Route, a difficult 67.4 miles (108.4 km) path. This route is known for its substantial elevation gain, traversing varied Cantabrian landscapes. Local road cyclists also love the Palombera Pass – Collada de Carmona loop from Cabezón de la Sal, a 55.1 miles (88.6 km) trail leading through mountain passes and scenic valleys, often completed in about 4 hours 47 minutes. Road cycling around Ruente is defined by green mountains, river valleys, and high plateaus. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging climbs. The routes in Ruente are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 19 reviews. More than 1,700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Ruente's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Ruente?
A: There are nearly 40 road cycling routes available around Ruente, offering a wide range of options for cyclists. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 19 reviews.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Ruente?
A: Road cycling around Ruente offers diverse terrain, characterized by green mountains, lush forests, and open meadows. The region, part of the Saja-Besaya Natural Park, features significant elevation changes and winding roads through protected natural areas, including routes along the Saja River and across high plateaus.

Q: Are there road cycling routes for different skill levels in Ruente?
A: Yes, Ruente offers road cycling routes for all skill levels. You'll find approximately 4 easy routes, 14 moderate routes, and 21 difficult routes, ensuring options for beginners to experienced cyclists looking for a challenge.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes in Ruente?
A: Absolutely. Ruente is known for its challenging routes, with 21 classified as difficult. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004913144/port-of-palombera-and-alto-de-san-cipriano-from-ruente-circular-through-the-saja-besaya-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port of Palombera and Alto de San Cipriano from Ruente&lt;/a&gt; is a circular route through the Saja-Besaya Natural Park, covering over 111 km with significant climbs. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005116747/los-10-000-del-soplao-road-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los 10,000 del Soplao Road Route&lt;/a&gt;, known for its substantial elevation gain across varied Cantabrian landscapes.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Ruente?
A: Yes, many routes around Ruente are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004913144/port-of-palombera-and-alto-de-san-cipriano-from-ruente-circular-through-the-saja-besaya-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port of Palombera and Alto de San Cipriano from Ruente&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through the Saja-Besaya Natural Park.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along road cycling routes in Ruente?
A: Road cycling routes in Ruente offer access to stunning natural features and landmarks. You can explore areas like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4566932" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Moral Plateau&lt;/a&gt;, which offers breathtaking landscapes, or pass by the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4243290" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fallen Oak of Brujilices&lt;/a&gt;. The Saja River flows through Ruente, and its upper and middle courses are protected, offering scenic views. The region is also home to the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3392395" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Esteban Hermitage (Monte Corona)&lt;/a&gt;, providing panoramic views from atop Monte Corona.

Q: Are there any waterfalls accessible near road cycling routes in Ruente?
A: While not directly on every road cycling route, the Lamiña Waterfalls are found near Barcenillas, within a beautiful beech forest, offering a tranquil spot for relaxation and a worthwhile detour for cyclists in the area.

Q: Is it possible to find family-friendly road cycling routes in Ruente?
A: Yes, Ruente offers some easier routes suitable for families, with approximately 4 routes classified as easy. These routes typically feature less elevation gain and shorter distances, providing a more relaxed cycling experience through the scenic landscape.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Ruente?
A: The region of Cantabria, including Ruente, is often considered a 'cycling paradise.' While specific weather data for Ruente isn't provided, the area's lush green plants and diverse terrain are enjoyable throughout much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the peak heat of summer or potential winter conditions in higher elevations.

Q: Are there specific regulations for cycling within the Saja-Besaya Natural Park?
A: Ruente is situated within the Saja-Besaya Natural Park, a protected area. While road cycling on public roads is generally permitted, it's always advisable to check local regulations for specific trails or forest tracks within the park, especially regarding off-road cycling or areas designated for special conservation. No specific permits for road cycling are mentioned.

Q: Can I find cafes, pubs, or accommodation in Ruente village?
A: Yes, Ruente village, with its traditional mountain-style houses, offers amenities for visitors. You can expect to find cafes, pubs, and accommodation options within the village to refuel and rest after your rides.

Q: Is public transport available to Ruente for cyclists with bikes?
A: The region of Cantabria has a cycling network that includes greenways and various routes. While specific bus or train services to Ruente with bike carriage are not detailed, it's common for regional transport services in cycling-friendly areas to accommodate bicycles. It's recommended to check with local bus or train operators for their current policies on bike transport to Ruente.

Q: Where can I park my car when starting a road cycling route in Ruente?
A: Ruente village serves as a convenient starting point for many routes. While specific parking facilities for cyclists are not detailed, you can typically find parking within the village or at designated trailheads, especially for popular routes. It's advisable to look for public parking areas upon arrival.
tips: Falangist monument located in the Saja Reserve at an altitude of 830 m above sea level.
The Palombera pass is a 1st category mountain pass located at the eastern end of the Sierra del Cordel. One of the most beautiful ports in Cantabria with a spectacular landscape.
Lantueno is a crossing point between Torrelavega and Reinosa with a natural and fresh water source ideal for recovering liquid.

The pre-Roman stelae of Barros and Lombera stand out, as well as the manor houses that can be found in the Rasilla park area, such as the palace of the Counts of Mansilla, Bustamante and Quijano. One of the main attractions are the Cantabrian Wars festivities, which are held on the last weekend of August and the first of September, recreating the wars between the Romans and the Cantabrians.
